claim: CRYS launches from the coding-agent folder with 'python -m crys /path/to/project' (or no path for the current directory); 'python -m crys' and 'python -m crystal_code' are identical aliases. On Windows Git Bash, use forward slashes or quote the path — backslashes in an unquoted path get eaten by the shell.

CRYS has NO '--prompt' flag. A one-shot instruction from a script or terminal is '--task "..."', which runs headless: plan pass, then auto-approved execution on a fresh git branch, then CLI-run verification, then a report. Never invent flags when generating scripts that invoke CRYS — the real surface is: --task, --branch, --queue, --daemon, --tasks, --db, --customer, --project, --ingest, --exclude, --code-only, --list-customers, -v.

'--task' headless runs require the project to be a git repository with a CLEAN worktree (uncommitted changes are refused so the user's work cannot end up in the agent's commit). The run creates its own branch (--branch NAME, default agent/task-<timestamp>) and restores the user's branch at the end no matter what.

'--db PATH_OR_URL' points CRYS at a knowledge store: a SQLite file path or a full SQLAlchemy URL such as Postgres; the default is crystal_cache.db in the launch folder. '--customer ID' works as an existing customer in that store with their full memory scope; the default is the local coding-agent customer. '--list-customers' is the diagnostic listing.

Queued and scheduled CRYS tasks execute ONLY while a daemon is running ('python -m crys --daemon', one per database, Ctrl+C to stop). Queueing without a running daemon stores the task but nothing will execute it — always tell the user whether a daemon is running when queueing, and how to start one.

Task scheduling accepts 'at' as a local time 'HH:MM' (meaning the next occurrence of that time) or 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM', and 'every' as an interval: '30m', '4h', '1d', '2w', or the words 'hourly'/'daily'/'weekly', with a 60-second minimum because each occurrence is a full agent run. 'every' without 'at' anchors the series one interval from now.

CRYS recurrence is fixed-rate against the wall clock, anchored at the start time: occurrences fire at anchor + k*interval, never finish-time plus interval. Missed occurrences SKIP — if the daemon was down three days, the task runs once at the next future slot, not three times in a burst. Times are local machine time. A recurring series parks after 3 consecutive failures instead of burning budget forever.

Approving a recurring queue_task once covers ALL future occurrences of that series — each occurrence is a separate full headless run. When proposing a recurring task, state this explicitly so the user understands the scope of their single approval.

The background queue is inspected with '/tasks' inside the REPL or 'python -m crys --tasks' from the shell — newest first with statuses (queued/running/done/failed) and any knowledge gaps awaiting retry. Per-task full logs live in ~/.crystal-code/tasks/<id>.log; the get_task_status tool reads one task or lists the 10 most recent.

In the CRYS REPL, '/auto' toggles session auto-approval for file writes and browser interactions ONLY — shell commands are ALWAYS approved individually, with no approve-all, and browser use always gets one explicit session consent. block_paths rules from .crystal-code.json hold in every mode including /auto.

'/plan' puts the CRYS agent in read-only investigation mode where it proposes a numbered plan (writes, shell, and browser interactions denied; reading and run_verify allowed); '/go' executes the proposed plan and leaves plan mode.

CRYS snapshots files before each approved write; '/checkpoints' lists this session's snapshots and '/rewind [n]' undoes the agent's file changes back n checkpoints (default 1). Rewind works on git projects only.

'/login' connects the REPL to a shared knowledge store with a customer API key (asks for DB path and key, confirms the customer, rebuilds the agent on that memory); '/logout' returns to the local default store and local customer. '/info' shows where everything stands: project, store, customer, approvals, shell and browser state, models, and connected hands.

'/ingest' runs a wizard that scans the project git-aware, previews counts and cost shape, then crystallizes files into the knowledge bank; re-runs are idempotent and skip unchanged files. Headless: '--ingest' (with optional repeatable '--exclude GLOB' and '--code-only' to skip prose, since code never costs LLM calls but prose extraction does).

CRYSTAL.md in the project folder holds standing rules the agent must follow; the agent creates or appends to it when the user sets a rule, and it is loaded at session start ('project instructions found') and obeyed in headless background runs too.

CRYS background/daemon runs are fully non-interactive by design: writes auto-approved ONLY on the fresh agent branch, shell and browser denied outright (nobody is watching), block_paths enforced, verification run by the CLI itself as ground truth rather than trusting the agent's claim, and the user's original branch restored no matter what.

A single write_file call is bounded by the model's output token budget; a large file emitted all at once gets cut off mid-call and the write fails silently. Write large files in sections: create the file with the first portion via write_file, then append each remaining section with its own edit_file call. Never retry the same oversized single write — split it.

key: General|CRYS|Files|Merge config files do not clobber
claim: When changing a config or manifest file (requirements.txt, package.json, pyproject.toml, etc.), READ it first and merge your change into the existing content; never overwrite the whole file with only your additions — that deletes other dependencies, comments, and settings the user needs. Surgical edits over whole-file rewrites.

When you pivot approach, delete the files the abandoned approach created instead of leaving them behind — stray generator scripts, dead drafts, and duplicate outputs become confusing corpses. And do not write a generator/build script to produce a file you can write directly with write_file; write the file itself.
